does: This paper develops a formal verification framework for guardrail classifiers that protect language models from harmful inputs. Rather than verifying safety in the discrete input space where "harmful" lacks formal definition, the authors shift verification to the pre-activation representation space of classifiers. They define harmful regions as convex shapes enclosing representations of known harmful prompts, then exploit the monotonicity of sigmoid classification heads to certify worst-case points efficiently in O(d) time without approximation. The approach yields closed-form soundness proofs and proposes two concrete constructions: SVD-aligned hyper-rectangles producing exact certificates and Gaussian Mixture Models producing probabilistic certificates over semantically coherent clusters of harmful content.
connects: This work addresses fundamental limitations in evaluating guardrail classifiers highlighted by red-teaming and adversarial testing literature, which revealed that classifiers passing empirical evaluations lack formal guarantees. The paper builds on established formal verification techniques from robustness certification and adversarial ML, particularly epsilon-ball perturbation analysis and convex relaxation methods, but adapts these by recognizing that semantic perturbations in LLM space don't correspond to small input-space changes. It also leverages representation space analysis from mechanistic interpretability research, using learned embeddings as the meaningful space for safety properties rather than raw text, combining these threads into a novel verification approach.
enables: This framework enables production deployment of guardrails with formal safety assurances, moving beyond heuristic confidence scores toward provable guarantees. The approach suggests future work in tightening the gap between probabilistic and deterministic certificates, extending verification to multi-stage safety pipelines, and scaling certification to larger representation spaces and more complex classifier architectures. It also opens directions for defining and certifying other semantic properties beyond harmfulness, establishing principled methods for safety specification in language models generally, and potentially combining formal verification with continual learning as new harmful behaviors emerge.
